title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita, 2010

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot compares per capita plastic waste generation against GDP per capita for countries in 2010, with circle sizes representing population. The horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale, ranging from $1,000 to over $100,000. The vertical axis measures plastic waste in kilograms per person per day, spanning from 0 kg to 0.6 kg. Data points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania.

The distribution shows a general positive relationship where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher per capita plastic waste, though the pattern contains significant variation. At the lower end of the income spectrum, countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo, Togo, and Tanzania cluster below $3,000 GDP per capita with plastic waste rates under 0.05 kg per person per day. Moving toward middle-income levels, India and Indonesia appear as large circles near $5,000 GDP with waste rates around 0.05 kg, while China sits near $10,000 GDP with waste around 0.15 kg.

At higher income levels, the United States appears as a large circle near $50,000 GDP with waste around 0.35 kg. European countries including Germany, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom cluster between $35,000 and $45,000 GDP with waste rates ranging from 0.2 kg to 0.5 kg. The highest waste rates appear among smaller nations: Kuwait and Antigua and Barbuda both exceed 0.6 kg per person per day, while Guyana reaches approximately 0.6 kg at a GDP near $10,000. Several high-income countries including Sweden, Canada, and Japan show waste rates between 0.1 kg and 0.2 kg, lower than many middle-income nations.

The data shows that while wealthier nations generally produce more plastic waste per person, the relationship is not uniform across all countries or regions.
